other_observers: Nathan Pieplow; Not sure who found it, but many people observed the bird.
specific_location: on Shadow Mountain Reservoir near the canal connecting with Grand Lake
description: The birds were very large (a lot larger than nearby Canada Geese, but significantly smaller than the nearby adult Trumpeter Swan), with the distinctive swan shape with a long neck and small head with a large bill.   The bodies of the birds were white overall, with a few dusky feathers mixed in on the back and wings, but not very many. The overall impression was of a white back. The necks where brown, darkest on the head and palest towards the body, with imm. number 1 having more brown on the neck below the head than imm. number 2.   The feet where black, and the bill on one bird (imm. number 1) was mostly black with a tiny bit of pink in the middle, and the bill of the other (imm. number 2) was mostly pink, with only a little bit of black at the ends. The culmen of imm. number 1 was slightly concaved. The culmen of imm. number 2 was more concave than that of the other immature, though not very much so. The length of the bill was about equal to that of the head. The eyes were distinct from the bill on both birds, more so on imm. number 2.   The heads were rounded, with the peak of the head in the middle of the head, and the cheek contour with the bill was not a smooth curve, but rather had notches in it, and it went straight down near the gape. The lengths of the necks were shorter than the body. The swans were significantly smaller than an adult Trumpeter Swan they were with.Behaviors: The two swans were loosely associated with an adult Trumpeter Swan on the lake, though the immatures tended stay together, and were not always with the adult. I never saw one immature far from the other.Call: none hearsPlumage: first basic molting into first alternate
photographer: Photographer: Andrew Spencer, Andrew Spencer, Andrew Spencer, Andrew Spencer
